text: BARLBOROUGH, a parish in Derbyshire, 8 m. NE of Chesterfield. Area 2,229 acres. Pop. 804.
BARLBY, a chapelry in Hemingborough p., in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 1½ m. NE of Selby. Area 1,310 acres. Pop. 387.
BARLEBEN, a town of Prussian Saxony, in the prov. of Magdeburg, 4 m. N of Neustadt.

BARLENGAS, or BERLENGAS, a cluster of rocky islets off the coast of Portugal, 5 m. NW of Cape Carvoeira. There is a lighthouse on the SE side of the Great B., with a revolving light elevated 365 ft. above sea-level, in N lat. 39° 25', W long. 9° 30' 17".
BARLESTON, a chapelry in the p. of Market- Bosworth, Leicestershire, 21 m. NE of Market-Bosworth. Area 810 acres. Pop. 580.

BARLE'TTA, a port of Naples, in the Terra di Bari, 43 m. ESE of Foggia, in N lat. 41° 20', E long. 16° 18'. The town is well-built, and the streets are wide and well-paved. The cathedral is a Gothic building of elegant exterior, with a high steeple. A rampart runs all round the town, between the walls and the houses; and a magnificent gateway communicates with the harbour, which is formed by a mole running out from the shore, and a narrow island on which a lighthouse is placed. A well-constructed citadel commands the port from the SE extremity of the town. B. carries on considerable commerce with Great Britain in wheat, and with the Ionian islands as well as in the gulf of Venice; and communicates with Naples by an excellent road. Its climate is mild in winter; but the vicinity of saltpits, only 7 m. to the N, and a continued series of marshes, extending in the same direction, render it insalubrious during the hot months. Pop. 20,000.
BARLEY, a parish in Hertfordshire, 2 m. NE of Barkway. Area 2,700 acres. Pop. 792.—Also a joint township with Wheatley, in the p. of Whalley in Lancashire. Area 2,370 acres. Pop. 686.
BARLEYTHORPE, a chapelry in the p. of Oakham-Deansfield, Rutlandshire, 2 m. NW of Oakham. Pop. 200.
BARLICHWAY, a hundred in the co. of Warwick, comprising the divisions of Alcester, Henley, Snitterfield, and Stratford. Area 100,310 acres. Pop. in 1831, 26,685; in 1841, 28,961.
BARLING, a parish in Essex, 3 m. ENE of Prittlewell. Area 1,240 acres. Pop. 326.
BARLINGS, a parish in Lincolnshire, 6 m. ENE of Lincoln. Area, with Langworth township, 2,630 acres. Pop. 352. BAR
BARLIEU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cher, cant. of Vailly. Pop. 942.
BARLOW, a township in the p. of Brayton, W. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. SE of Selby. Area 2,150 acres. Pop. 284.—Also a township in Hopesay p., Salop.
BARLOW (GREAT), a township in the p. of Staveley, Derbyshire, 4 m. NW of Chesterfield. Area 3,760 acres. Pop. 627.
BARLOW (LITTLE), a hamlet in the p. of Dronfield, Derbyshire, 5 m. NW of Chesterfield. Pop. 59.
BARMACHI, a village of Albania, in the sanjak of Toli Monaster, 22 m. NE of Premiti.
BARMAIR, or BARMER, a town of Hindostan, in Rajasthan, 5 m. NE of Junna.
BARMBECK, a town in the territory of Hamburg. Pop. 1,600.
BARMBY-ON-THE-MARSH, a chapelry in the p. of Howden, E. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SW of Howden. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 506.
BARMBY-ON-THE-MOOR, a p. in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. W of Pocklington. Area 2,290 acres. Pop. 475.

BARMEN, a district and town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, and circle of Elberfeld, lying along the Wüpper or Wipper. It is connected by a bridge with Elberfeld, and the whole forms an almost continuous line of houses, shops, and manufactories, for upwards of 6 m. The pop. of B., including Gemarke, Rittershausen, Wichlinghausen, and Heckinghausen, was 28,975 in 1837; in 1817, 19,171. The chief manufactures are silks, lace, linen, stockings, and iron ware; and the joint industry of the whole district is said to amount to £2,000,000 annually.
BARMING, a parish in Kent, 2 m. W by S of Maidstone. Area 1,210 acres. Pop. 584.

BARMOUTH, or ABERMAW-locally BERMAWa town of Merionethshire, in the p. of Llanater, 8 m. SW of Dolgelly, at the mouth of the river Maw or Mawddach, more commonly known by the name of Afon, which is navigable by small boats nearly as high up as Dolgelly. It lies at the foot of a lofty mountain, and is built on a steep bank, the houses forming successive terraces, and being approached by steps cut in the rock. Pop. 930. The town is much frequented during summer for sea-bathing. On the banks of the river Maw, and on the sands at low water, there are agreeable promenades; and the view of the distant mountains is extremely picturesque. Sergeant Talfourd does not hesitate to compare the scenery in the neighbourhood with that of the farfamed Drachenfels and the Rhine, and to assign the palm to the landscape between Dolgelly and A. "Let any one," says he, "who has knowledge of the two scenes, call to memory that glorious estuary reposing in beauty and crowded with grandeur; look down its avenue of sparkling water to the distant sea, glistening in the western light; let him glance on the one side at its curving shore of oak-sprinkled meadows edged and broken by rock, and on the other to the pillared precipices of Cadir Idris, and then, with all respect to the noble substance of the flowing Rhine, gaze at its vine-spotted banks, and say if there is the faintest approach to rivalry." It has a considerable trade in flannel and hosiery; and there are upwards of 100 small sloops belonging to the place.
BARMOUTH CREEK, a small bay on the coast of New South Wales, in S lat. 36° 42', E long. 150° 4'.
BARMPTON, a township in the p. of Haughtonle-Skerne, in Durham, 4 m. NE of Darlington. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 124.
BARMSTEDT, a town of Denmark, in the duchy of Holstein, on the Elmshornerau river, or Kruckau, 6 m. NW of Elmshorn. Pop. 700.
BARMSTON, a township in the p. of Washington, in Durham, 5 m. W of Sunderland. Area 980 acres. Pop. 81.—Also a p. in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. S by W of Bridlington. Area 2,180 acres. Pop. 254.

BARN HILL, a promontory on the Abyssinian coast of the Red sea, 26 m. W of Ras Billul.-Also a piece of table land on the shore of the Persian gulf, in N lat. 27° 11'.—Also a hill in S Australia, on the E side of Spencer's gulf, under the meridian of 138° 30′ E.
BARN MOUNTAINS, the N extremity of Richardson chain, in N lat. 68° 40′, W long. 137° 50′.
BARNABE' (SAINT), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, com. of Marseilles. Pop. 1,026.—Also a com. in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of La Cheze. Pop. 1,047.
BARNACK, a parish in Northamptonshire, 4 m. N of Wansford. Area 4,440 acres. Pop., with the hamlets of Pilsgate and Southorpe, 860.
BARNACLE, a hamlet in the parishes of Bulkington and Shilton, in Warwickshire, 4 m. SE of Nuneaton. Pop. 263.
BARNACRE, a township in the p. of Garstang, in Lancashire, 2 m. NE of Garstang. Area 3,880 acres. Pop., with Bonds, 628.
BARNAGASHI. See Bahr-Negash.
BARNAGEE, a ridge of mountains in co. Mayo, extending E and W, about 2 m. S of Lough Conn.
BARNAGORE, a village of Hindostan, 21 m. N of Calcutta, on the l. bank of the Hoogly.
BARNAHELY, a parish in co. Cork, 9 m. SE of Cork, on the W shore of Cork harbour. Area 895 acres. Pop. 1,183.
BARNANE, a parish in co. Tipperary, 2 m. W by N of Templemore. Area 2,167 acres. Pop. 752.
BARNARD, a town in Windsor co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 10 m. N of Woodstock. Pop. 1,774.—Also a village in Piscataquis co., Maine, U. S. Pop. 153.

BARNARD, a river in South Australia, which Major Mitchell found flowing to the NW, in 37° S lat., and 144° 15' E long., between bold banks, those on the left consisting of bold rocks of granite; while those on the r. were of trap. BARNARD CASTLE,—as frequently Castle- Barnard,—a market-town and chapelry in the p. of Gainford, co.-palatine of Durham, 246 m. NNW of London, and 26 m. SW of Durham. Area of chapelry, 3,810 acres. The town, which consists of a spacious street nearly 1 m. in length, intersected by smaller streets, is situated on the S side of an eminence rising abruptly from the N bank of the river Tees, over which there is a narrow bridge of two pointed arches. The streets are well-paved and lighted with gas. The environs present romantic scenery, especially along the banks of the Tees. Hats, carpets, and white leather-breeches, are made here; on the banks of the Tees are several mills for spinning thread; and one of the largest corn-markets in the North of England is held here weekly. B. is one of the polling places for South Durham. The town, which wholly belongs to the Duke of Cleveland, is governed by a steward and jury of the manor of Darlington. The remains of the castle are extremely interesting, and cover six acres of land. Pop. in 1801, 2,966; in 1831, 4,430; in 1841, 4,452. A railway has been proposed to join B. with the Stockton and Darlington railway.
BARNARD ISLE, a small islet off the NE coast of Australia, in S lat. 17° 55'; E long. 144° 5'.
BARNARDISTON, a parish in Suffolk, 4 m. WNW of Clare. Area 1,520 acres. Pop. 237.

BARNAUL, a district and town of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Tomsk, on the Barnaulka, at its fall into the Obi, in N lat. 53° 20', E long. 83° 27'; at an alt. of 366 ft. above the level of the Caspian. It has BAR a pop. of about 12,000; and is the seat of administration for the whole mining-district of Kolyvan. It is well and regularly built, on a spot considerably lower than the level of the surrounding country, which is thickly clothed with forests of pine and fir. All the gold from the Altai and Eastern Siberia, is brought here to be melted into ingots preparatory to its being forwarded to St. Petersburg; and in the neighbourhood are a number of silver-mines, the property of the Crown, the annual produce of which, amounting to about 250 poods of silver, and about 1,000,000 poods of lead, copper, and iron, is smelted at B. There is a fine mineralogical museum here; and a good school of engineering; also manufactories of paper, glass, and coarse ticking.
BARNBOW, a hamlet in the p. of Barwick-in- Elmet, W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. ENE of Leeds. Coal-mines and slate-quarries are wrought in the vicinity.
BARNBROUGH, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. W of Doncaster. Area 1,770 acres. Pop., with the hamlet of Harlington, 508.
BARNBY, a parish in Suffolk, 5 m. E by S of Beccles. Area 1,390 acres. Pop. 296.—Also a township in the p. of Lythe, N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. W by N of Whitby. Area 1,400 acres. Pop. 262.
BARNBY-UPON-DON, a parish and township in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. NNE of Doncaster. Area, including the township of Thorpe, 3,670 acres. Pop. 629, of whom 510 are in the township of B.
BARNBY-MOOR, a township in the p. of Blyth, in Nottinghamshire, 4 m. NW of East Retford. Pop. 221.
BARNBY-IN-THE-WILLOWS, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 4 m. E by S of Newark. Area 1,690 acres. Pop. 266.
BARNE, a reported large inland town of Eastern Africa, 15 days' journey from Tette on the Zambezi, which is 60 leagues beyond Senna.
BARNEGAT, a village in Dutchess co, in the state of New York, U. S., on the E side of Hudson's river. Pop. 180.—Also a v. in Monmouth co., New Jersey.

BARNEGAT BAY, an inlet on the coast of New Jersey, U. S., 74 m. ENE of Cape May. It is separated from the ocean by Island Beach and Squam Beach; and runs up 20 m. to Metetecunk river, with a breadth of from 1 to 4 m.

BARNES, a parish and v. in Surrey, pleasantly situated on the banks of the Thames, 5 m. SW of London, and within a short distance of Mortlake. Area of p. 820 acres. The v. consists of a few straggling houses opposite a common; a small street leading to the Thames; and a row of elegant houses facing the river. Barnes-common consists of about 500 acres of ground, which are in great part flooded by the Thames in spring-tides. Pop. in 1801, 860; in 1831, 1,417; in 1841, 1,461.
BARNESLEY. See BARNSLY.
BARNESVILLE, a town in Belmont co., Ohio, U. S., 101 m. E of Columbus. Pop. 700.
BARNET, a township in Caledonia co., in the state of Vermont, 10 m. S by E of Danville, on Connecticut river, at the Fifteen miles falls. Slate and iron ore are wrought in this township. Pop. 2,030.
BARNET-CHIPPING, or BERGNET-CHIPPING, or High-Barnet, but most commonly Chipping-

BARNET, a market-town and parish in Hertfordshire, 11 m. NNW of London, and 9 m. SE of St. Albans, on the great post-road betwixt these cities. Area of p. 1,440 acres. It consists principally of one street a m. in length. A very large and important fair is held here on the 4th and 5th of Sept., for the sale of cattle and horses, which are collected in the north of England, and in Scotland, in the early part of the X spring. In the fairs for 1839, upwards of 45,000 head of cattle, and 1,000 horses changed owners. Pop. in 1801, 1,258; in 1831, 2,369; in 1841, 2,485.
BARNET-EAST, a parish in Hertfordshire, adjoining the above. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 598.
BARNET-FRIAM, a parish in Middlesex, 9 m. NW of London. Area 1,330 acres. Pop., with Colney-Hatch and part of Whetstone, 849.
BARNETBY-LE-WOLD, a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. ENE of Glandford-Brigg. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 679.
BARNEVELD, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Guelderland, 20 m. NW of Arnheim. Pop. 4,000.
BARNEVELT ISLANDS, a group of small islands NE of Cape Horn, in S lat. 55° 49', W long. 66° 40'.
BARNEVILLE-SUR-MER, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Manche, arrond. of Valognes. The cant. comprises 16 com. The com. and town are 18 m. SW of Valognes. Pop. 1,083.
BARNEY, a parish in Norfolkshire, 5 m. ENE of Fakenham. Area 1,210 acres. Pop. 276.
BARNHAM, a parish in Suffolk, 3 m. S of Thetford. Area 5,420 acres. Pop. 412.—Also a p. in Sussex, 4 m. WSW of Arundel. Area 730 acres. Pop. 125.
BARNHAM-BROOM, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. NW of Wymondham. Area 1,870 acres. Pop. 494.

BARNINGHAM, a parish and township in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. NW of Richmond. Area of p. 10,930 acres, including the townships of B., Hope, Scarsgill, and part of Newsham. Pop. of p. 600; of t. 337.—Also a parish in Suffolk, 8 m. SW of E Harling. Area 1,520 acres. Pop. 508.—Also a p. in Norfolk, 5 m. NW of Aylesham. Area 770 acres. Pop. 329.
BARNINGHAM-NORWOOD, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. E by S of Holt. Area 810 acres. Pop. 54.
BARNINGHAM-PARVA, a parish adjacent to the above. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 229.
BARNINGHAM-WINTER, a parish in Norfolk, 53 m. SE of Holt. Area 860 acres. Pop. 86.
BARNOLDBY-LE-BECK, a parish in Lincolnshire, 6 m. SW of Grimsby. Area 1,460 acres. Pop. 292.

BARNOLDSWICK, or GILL, a parish and township in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. N of Colne. Area, including the townships of B., Brogden, Coates, and Saltersforth, 6,040 acres. Pop. 2,844. Area of t. 2,020 acres. Pop. 1,849.
BARNS (East and West), two nearly adjacent villages in the p. of Dunbar, East Lothian, 2 m. E of Dunbar. Pop. of both, 295.

BARNSLEY, or BARNESLEY, a chapelry and market-town in the p. of Silkstone, W. R. of Yorkshire, 172 m. NNW of London, 40 m. SW of York, and 10 m. S of Wakefield, on the post-road to Sheffield; and on the line of the North Midland railway, by which it is <math>19\frac{3}{4}</math> m. from Leeds, and <math>53\frac{1}{4}</math> m. from Derby. Area of c. 4,000 acres. The town is situated on the side of a hill, and consists of several narrow streets, in which are some handsome houses, and a spacious market-place. The houses are in general built of stone obtained from quarries in the neighbourhood. The inhabitants were formerly much employed in wire-drawing, but this branch of business has declined; and the spinning of yarn and weaving of tinen-fabrics, especially damasks and drills, now employs a number of hands. Coal is found in abundance in the neighbourhood, the mining of which gives employment to 550 men; and there are a glasswork, several dye-works, and three iron foundries. The Dearne and Dove canal passes near the town; and the Barnsley canal communicates with Wakefield by the Calder. This canal was opened in 1799; BAR it crosses the Dearne by an aqueduct of 5 arches, and is connected with a railroad to Silkstone. An act has been obtained for a railway, about 9 m. in length, connecting the Sheffield and Manchester railway at Penistone with the N. Midland railway at Cudworth bridge, near B. Pop. in 1801, 3,606; in 1831, 10,330; in 1841, 12,310.
BARNSLEY, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4 m. ENE of Cirencester. Area 2,050 acres. Pop. 305.
BARNSMORE, a group of mountains in co. Donegal, ranging E and W between the baronies of Tyrhugh and Raphoe. They attain in some places an alt. of 1,000 ft.

BARNSTABLE, a county in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., including the whole of Cape Cod, 65 m. in length, with a medium breadth of 5 m. A great portion of the district is sandy and incapable of cultivation; and the inhabitants chiefly depend upon their cod and mackerel fisheries. Pop. in 1840, 32,548.—The cap. of the same name, is 74 m. SE of Boston, on the S side of a bay opening into Cape Cod bay. The tonnage of this port in 1840 was 156,566 tons. Pop. 4,301.

BARNSTAPLE, a port, borough, market-town, and parish, in the hund. of Braunton, co. of Devon; 192 m. W by S of London, and 37 m. NW of Exeter. Area of p. 1,070 acres. The town is situated about 6 m. from the mouth of the Taw, on the E bank of the river, over which there is a stone-bridge of 16 arches, in a pleasant valley bounded by a semicircular range of hills, to which the river forms a chord. The streets are regular; and, generally speaking, B. is one of the most agreeable towns in the county. Its commerce is still considerable though much less than formerly in consequence of the obstruction which has been long arising to the navigation by the accumulation of sand in the river. The number of sailing vessels registered at this port in 1845 was 52 under 50 tons, and 34 above that burden; total tonnage 4,547 tons. Its trade consists chiefly in the importation of wood from North America and the Baltic, and lime from Wales, and in coasting-traffic with the various ports in the Bristol channel. The chief local trade is that of malting. The most important manufactures are those of serge and shalloons, and patent lace; there are several tanyards, an iron-foundry, and extensive works for the manufacture of coarse pottery, bricks, and tiles. Lead-ore has been found in the neighbourhood, and limestone is abundant. The weekly market is celebrated for extensive dealings in corn and provisions. An act has been obtained for a railway to connect B. with the Bristol and Exeter railway, at Crediton, and by a branchline with South Molton. B. has had a commission of the peace granted, and a court of quarter-sessions and recorder appointed. The limits of the borough are co-extensive with those of the town and parish. The town has returned two members to parliament since the 23° Edward I. The mayor is the returning officer. It is also a polling-place for the members for South Devon. Pop. in 1801, 3,748; in 1831, 6,840; in 1841, 7,902; of the parl. borough, 10,259. Number of electors in 1832, 720; in 1842_3, 762.— B. is said to have been erected into a borough by Athelstan, who built a castle here at the junction of the Taw with the Yeo, of which no vestiges now remain except the artificial mount on which it stood. It was incorporated by Henry I. It was formerly a place of considerable trade. In 1588, it fitted out three ships for the fleet raised to repel the Spanish armada; and, during the parliamentary wars, it was distinguished for its opposition to the royal cause.
BARNSTEAD, a township in Belknap co., in New Hampshire, U. S., 18 m. NE of Concord. Pop. 1,945.

BARNSTON, a township in the p. of Woodchurch in Cheshire, 4 m. N of Great Neston. Area 1,130 acres. Pop. 206. The Grand Trunk canal here passes through a tunnel 550 yds. in length.-Also a township in Essex, 2 m. SE of Dunmow. Area 2,770 acres. Pop. 197.-Also a chapelry in Langar p., Nottinghamshire. Pop. 136.
BARNSTEDT, a village of Prussian Saxony, in the prov. of Merseburg, SE of Querfurt. Pop. 500.
BARNSTORF, a village of Hanover, in the circle and NE of Diepholz, on the Hunte. Pop. 700.
BARNTON, a township in the p. of Great Budworth, Cheshire, 2 m. NW of Northwich. Area 630 acres. Pop. 839.
BARNTRUP, a town in the principality of Lippe- Detmold, 3 m. NNE of Blomberg. Pop. 1,000.

BARNWELL, a district in the SW part of the state of South Carolina, U.S., between the Savannah river and the Edisto. Its chief production is cotton. The Charleston and Hamburgh railroad passes through it. Pop. in 1840, 21,471.—Its cap. of the same name is situated on Turkey creek, 100 m. S by W of Columbia. Pop. 400.
BARNWELL-ALL-SAINTS, or King's Barn-
WELL, a parish of Northamptonshire, 3 m. SE of Oundle. Area 1,680 acres. Pop. 140.
BARNWELL-ST.-ANDREWS, a parish adjoining the above. Area 1,740 acres. Pop. 282.
BARNWOOD, a parish in Gloucestershire, 2 m. ESE of Gloucester. Area 1,150 acres. Pop. 383. conquhar, Fifeshire. Pop. 232.

BARO, a river of the Wallega country, to the S of Abyssinia, which was described to Dr. Beke as being a very large river, larger than the Abai, and as flowing NW, uniting with the Gaba, and thereafter joining the Abai. Its valley is said to be inhabited by Shankalas or Negroes. [Geo. Jour., vol. xiii.]—Also a town of Abyssinia, 40 m. NW of Dixan. BAROACH, BROACH, or BHAROUCH, a pergunnah of Gujerat, on the W side of the gulf of Cambay. Area 1,600 sq. m. It is a fertile and well-cultivated district, and yields a revenue of about £220,000. Rice and cotton are the chief productions. In 1814, the pop. amounted to 157,983; in 1828, to 229,500, of whom about three-fourths were Hindus.— The cap., bearing the same name, is situated on the Nerbudda, 25 m. above its mouth, in N lat. 21° 46', 220 m. from Bombay; and seems to correspond in site to the ancient Barygaza. It maintains a considerable commerce with Bombay and Surat by the Nerbudda. Its chief exports are raw cotton, muslins and chintzes, grain, seeds, and dye woods. In 1804, the pop. amounted to 22,468; in 1812, to 32,716, of whom 19,836 were Hindus. It was taken by the British in 1772, and again in 1782; and, with the prov., was finally ceded to them in 1803.
BAROCHE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of La Pontoye. Pop. 2,014.
BAROCHE-SOUS-LUCE' (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Juvigni-sur- Andaine. Pop. 1,545.

BARODA, or BARODAH, a city and district in the prov. of Gujerat. The city is situated in N lat. 22° 21', E long. 73° 23', 230 m. from Bombay, and 100 m. from Surat. Its pop. was estimated in 1818 at 100,000; and Sir John Malcolm, who visited it in 1830, reports that it was at that time one of the richest cities, in point of capital, of its extent in India. In 1803 and 1806 its bankers advanced £1,500,000 in ready money for the service of the British troops. Yet there are few outward signs of wealth about the place. The streets are wide but dirty, with high houses chiefly built of wood having tiled sloping roofs. The palace is a large but shabby building; and there are some tolerable pagodas. A British BAR subsidiary force, maintained at the expense of the rajah, occupy cantonments about 2 m. from the town, at which there is a neat church of Gothic architecture for their use. The guicowar or guicwar of B. is said to have an annual revenue of 80 lacs of rupees, or about £800,000; and his territory is considerable both in Cutch, Cuttewar, and Gujerat. The district is fertile, and well-cultivated in general; but much of its surface appears to be of a wild and jungly character. The wilder parts are occupied by Bheels; but Coolies form more than one-half of the pop.—A railroad is projected between the city of B. and the port of Tankaria on the gulf of Cambay. Its length will be about 40 m., of which 18 m. will lie in British territory, and 22 m. in that of the guicowar. The district to be traversed is a dead level.
BARONELLO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, on the r. bank of the Biferno, 6 m. NW of Bojano.
BARONSTOWN, a parish in co. Louth, 5 m. NW of Dundalk. Area 2,209 acres. Pop. 967.
BARONY, a township in the parish of Auckland- St.-Andrew, co.-palatine of Durham. Area 3,480 acres. Pop. 1,729.—See also Glasgow.
BARONDES, a mountain-peak of Spain, in the Pyrenean chain, at the head of the valley D'Aure, in N lat. 42° 44', E long. 0° 21. Alt. 9,787 ft.—Reboul and Vidal.
BAROU, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of

BARNYARDS, a village in the parish of Kil- | Gundwana, 13 m. SSE of Chowragur.—Also an island off the coast of Colombia, about 13 m. in length, whose N coast forms the S coast of the Boca Chica, or entrance to the harbour of Carthagena. It is separated from the main by a creek navigable by canoes only.
BAROUK, a Yezdehy district and village of Syria, in Lebanon, between Deir-el-Kamar and Zahle.
BAROUS, a town on the W coast of Sumatra. It exports benzoin, gold, and camphor.
BARQUIA, a town of Buenos Ayres, in the prov. of Entre Rios, near the l. bank of the Uruguay.
BARQUERO BAY, or BAY OF STANQUES LA
VERRE, an inlet on the coast of Galicia in Spain, on the E side of Punta de la Estaca, forming an excellent harbour 6 m. in length, and 3 m. wide.

BARQUISIMETO, a prov. of Venezuela, between 8° 47' and 10° 46' N lat.; and 1° 25' and 3° 25' W long. of Caracas. It is divided into the cantons of B., Carora, Quibor, San Felipe, Tocuyo, and Yaritagua. Pop. in 1841, 112,755. Its surface presents a succession of mountain-ridges belonging to the NE portion of the Andes, with large intervening valleys, and several extensive table-lands. Its staple products are cacao, coffee, sugar, indigo, salt, and fruits. —Its cap. of the same name is situated upon an elevated plain, in N lat. 9° 50', 120 m. WSW of Caracas. It contains about 12,000 inhabitants; but was dreadfully devastated by the earthquake of 1812.

BARR, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, arrond. of Schelestat, at the foot of the Vosges.—The cant. comprises 14 com.— The com. is 10 m. N of Schelestat. Pop. 4,514. The surrounding country is finely clothed with vineyards, and the v. is a considerable entrepot for wine, brandy, and grain.

BARR, a parish in the Carrick district of Ayrshire. Area 64,000 acres. Pop. in 1801, 742; in 1841, 959. It is a wild moorland district, of which not above one-fiftieth part is under cultivation, and not as much more cultivable. Some of the mountains on the banks of the Minnock attain an alt. of 2,700 ft.—The v. of B., situated at the influx of the Gregg into the Stinchar, has a pop. of about 250.

BARRA, or BARA, or not unfrequently BARRAY, an island-parish in the co. of Inverness, consisting of a group of islands divided, on the N, from South Uist by a channel of 8 m. The island of Tiree, in Argyle, the nearest land on the S, lies at the distance of about 35 m. The islands of Canna and Rum are at the distance of about 30 m. The parish consists of the main island of Barra, and a number of smaller islands and islets. The whole is reckoned to consist of 4,000 acres of arable land, and 18,000 of meadow and hill-pasture.—The main island of B. is 8 m. in length, and from 2 m. to 4 m. in breadth. It is deeply intersected in different places by arms of the sea. A low sandy isthmus, nearly covered at high, tide, divides it into two portions, the southern and larger of which rises to an alt. of 2,000 ft. It has a barren rocky appearance, excepting at the N end, which is fertile. In the middle and at the S end are some high hills. The soil in general is thin and rocky, and there is also a great deal of sand which is blown about with every gale of wind. According to Dr. Webster's report, the number of souls upon these islands in 1755 was 1,150; in 1801, it was 1,969; in 1831, 2,097, of whom 302 families were employed in agriculture, 15 in manufacture and handicraft, and 56 were not comprised in either of these classes. In 1841, the entire pop. of the parish was 2,363, of whom 1,977 were in the island of B. They speak the Gaelic language, and, in the opinion of some, with great purity. Considerable quantities of cod and ling are caught upon the E coast of B., from the mouth of Loch Boisdale to Barrahead. At the close of last cent. from 20 to 30 boats were generally employed in this fishery from the latter end of March, or the beginning of April, to the end of June; in 1829, the number of boats employed in the herring, cod, and ling fisheries was 81, manned by 405 hands. Shellfish, especially cockles, abound, and there are some small lochs which contain trout. The breed of ponies reared on this island are celebrated for their strength, symmetry, and agility. They are never stabled during the spring, summer, or autumn; and, even in winter, by turning up the ground with their hoofs, contrive to subsist on the roots of the grass.

BARRA, or BARR, a petty state of Western Africa, on the N bank of the Gambia, bounded by the Badibu territory on the E, and extending about 18 leagues along the coast, with a breadth of 14 leagues. This, and some of the neighbouring states, were founded by a Mandingo warrior. The Mandingo pop.—whom Golberry estimates at 200,000—are an industrious and comparatively civilized race. They are above the middle size, are well made, robust, and capable of bearing great fatigue. Their women also are stout, active, and good-looking. The clothes of both sexes are of cotton, which they manufacture themselves. The men wear drawers which hang half-way down the thigh, and an open tunic similar to our surplice; have sandals on their feet, and cotton caps on their heads. The women's dress consists of two pieces of linen, 6 ft. long and about 3 ft. wide; the one is plaited round the loins, and falls down to the ankle, forming a kind of petticoat; while the other negligently covers their bosom and shoulders. Their habitations, like those of all the other Negroes, are small and inconvenient huts. A mud wall about 4 ft. high, over which is a conical opening made of bamboos and straw, serves for the residence of the rich man as well as the slave. The furniture is equally rude: beds made of a bundle of reeds placed on pickets, and covered with a mat or an ox's skin, a jar for water, a few earthen vessels for boiling meat, with some wooden bowls, calabashes, and one or two stools, form the whole of their household goods. All the Mandingos in a free state have several wives; but they cannot marry two sisters. The women have each a hut; while all the hovels belonging to one I. BAR master are surrounded by a lattice-work of bamboo made with much art. An assemblage of this kind is called sirk or sourk. Several of these enclosures, separated by narrow paths, compose a town. The huts are placed according to the caprice of the person to whom they belong: the only point to which they uniformly attend is to have the door in a SW direction, that it may admit the sea-breezes. In each town a spot enclosed by interlaced reeds, and generally covered by trees which protect it from the sun, is set apart for the assemblies of the old men. Here they discuss public affairs, and try causes; the idle also resort hither to smoke their pipes and hear the news. They have also missourates or mosques, where they meet to say the prayers prescribed by the Koran. The pop. of the free Mandingos forms at the utmost about one-fourth of the inhabitants of the districts which they occupy on the Gambia; the remaining three-fourths are born in slavery, and are employed in all kinds of servile labour; but the free Mandingo has no right to take the life of his slave, nor to sell him to a foreigner, unless he has been publicly tried, and decreed to deserve such a punishment. Another part of the pop. of B. is composed of the descendants of the Portuguese families who remain in the country. Such persons, or rather those who take the title of Portuguese-for all the Mulattos, and even men who are almost black, call themselves Portuguese - profess the Catholic religion, and have churches and priests of their own. They are recognised by their costume: they wear a great chaplet suspended from the neck, a very long sword by their side, a shirt, a cloak, a hat, and a poniard. Some of these people devote themselves to commerce and agriculture, and they are generally adroit, brave, and enterprising. They navigate the river in canoes or boats, and generally perform such voyages on account of the merchants at the French factories, who intrust them with merchandise, and pay them liberally. M. Durand suggests that men so inured to the climate might advantageously be employed to travel over, and make discoveries in the interior of Africa. From Bunyadu creek, at the mouth of the Gambia, to Jukarda creek, in the B. territory, a line of coast 1 m. in breadth, and 42 m. in length, with the exception of a small spot occupied by the French at Albreda, was ceded in sovereignty to Britain by treaty with the king and chiefs of B., signed at Jillifree in June 1826.

BARRA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Espirito- Santo, on the r. bank of the estuary of the Cricaré or San Matheos, and 3 leagues SE of the town of the latter name.—Also the name of a fall in the Taquari river, in the Brazilian prov. of Matto-Grosso, in S lat. 18° 24', 2\frac{1}{2} m. below the confluence of the Sanguesuga. In travelling from San Paulo to Cuiaba, by the Taquari, it is necessary to make a portage here.— Also a town of Turkey in Bosnia, 33 m. SE of Bosna- Serai.—Also a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra 1ma, in the district of Reggio. Pop. 3,200.—Also a town of Naples, in the Terra di Lavora, 4 m. E of Naples. Pop. 5,000.
BARRA-DAS-EGOAS, a district and village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, lying along the Egoas, an affluent of the Paracatu. Pop. 1,000.
BARRA-LONGA, a district and village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, comarca of Ouro-Preto, on the Gualacho. Pop. 5,000.
BARRA-MANSA, a town and district of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, on the r. bank of the Parahiba. Pop. 6,000. A great deal of coffee is grown in this district.
BARRA-DE-ODEMIRA, an inlet on the coast of Alemtejo, in Portugal, forming also the embouchure of the Mira river. 2 P

BARRA-DO-RIO-DE-CONTAS, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, comarca of Ilheos, in Slat. 14° 18', on the S bank of the Contas. Pop. 3,000, chiefly Indians, who cultivate large quantities of mandioc and cocoa.
BARRA-DO-RIO-GRANDE, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, comarca of San Francisco, at the confluence of the Rio Grande with the San Francisco. It was annexed to Bahia in 1820. Pop. 4,000.
BARRA-DE-SAN-MIGUEL, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Alagoas, near the embouchure of the San Miguel.

BARRA-DO-RIO-NEGRO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, on the l. bank of the Rio Negro, 6 m. above its fall into the Amazon. Pop. 8,000, who support themselves by cultivating tobacco, making turtle-oil, salting fish, and growing coffee and cocoa.
BARRA-DO-RIO-DE-SAN JOANO, a district and village of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, near the town of Macahe. Pop. 4,000.
BARRA-SECCA, a district of Brazil, in the prov. of Espirito-Santo, comarca of San Matheos, between the Preto and the Santa Anna rivers on the W, and the ocean on the E. Pop. 9,000.
BARRA-DAS-VELHAS, a district of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 90 leagues N of Ouro- Preto, on the r. bank of the San Francisco. Pop. 3,000.
BARRABUTTI, a fortress of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, 1 m. NW of Cuttack. It is an old and decaying place, having been built in the 14th cent. It was taken by the British in 1803.

BARRACKPUR, a large cantonment or military village in Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, on the E side of the Hooghly, 24 m. by water from Calcutta. The river throughout this distance continues of nearly the same width as at Calcutta. It may be ascended to B. with a favourable tide and a good set of rowers, in 2½ hours; and descended in less than 2 hours. The governor-general has his country residence here, in a noble park of about 250 acres of fine turf, with spreading scattered trees, and a character quite European. Immediately in front, on the opposite side of the river, is seen the quiet-looking town of Serampore.
BARRACOE, a town of W Africa, between Acra and Winnebah.

BARRACONDA, or BARAKUNDA, a town of Senegambia, in the Wulli territory, near the confluence of the Wulli river with the Gambia, in N lat. 13° 28', W long. 13° 47'. The navigation of the Gambia is here interrupted by a ledge of rock, which runs across it, and creates a fall, or rather a rapid, for canoes can ascend it; but a few miles to the W of B. the tide rises from 3 to 4 ft.; and the river itself is as wide as the Thames at London-bridge.
BARRACONTA, an island off the SW point of Madagascar, in 25° 30' S lat.

BARRADA, or BARADA, a river of Syria, in the pash. of Damascus, which rises about 25 m. NW of the city of Damascus, and issuing from the mountains, divides into several streams, which water the environs of that city, while the centre or main branch flows directly past it. The greater part of the water of this river is employed for purposes of irrigation; but what is not thus consumed appears to unite again into one stream on the SE side of Damascus, and flowing towards the Desert, forms the Bahr-el-Marj, or 'Lake of the Meadow.' The waters of the B., like those of the Jordan, are of a whitish hue, and have an unpleasant taste. It is generally supposed to be the Pharpar of the Scriptures. In the upper part of its course, or the Wady B., this river flows through romantic rocky scenery, in some parts hemmed in by dark perpendicular cliffs; in others rushing over BAR broken and dissevered masses of rock; now overshadowed by trees, and flowing "gentle as infancy" between verdant banks; and again rushing with foamy current through rocky gorges, or precipitating itself in rapids and cascades.
BARRAFRANCA, a town of Sicily, in the district of Piazza. Pop. 5,800.

BARRAGAN, a name borne by the Orinoco in that part of its course which lies between the junction of the Meta and that of the Cazanare.-Also an ensanada, or careening place, on the Rio de la Plata, 9 leagues below Buenos Ayres, and 141 leagues above Magdalena.

BARRAGH, a parish in co. Carlow, 21 m. W by N of Clonegal. Area 13,296 acres. Pop. 3,742. Barragh-hill, near the centre of the p., has an alt. of 797 ft. Three mountains on the W boundary have altitudes respectively of 1,335 ft., 1,399 ft., and 1,499 ft.; and Mount Leinster, on the S boundary, attains an alt. of 2,610 ft.

BARRAGORANG, a long narrow valley in Camden co., New South Wales, between the Merrigong range and the Blue mountains, watered by the Warragamba, but almost excluded from the sun by the high rocky precipitous mountains which overhang each side of it.
BARRAGURRI, or BARAGHARI, a fort in the prov. of Nepal in India, 40 m. SE of Catmandu, in the district of Mukwanpur.
BARRA-INDING, a town of Senegambia, the cap. of the state of Barra, in N lat. 13° 25', W long. 16° 45'. An active commerce is carried on at this place in corn, cloth, gold dust, and ivory.

BARRAMAHAL, or BARRAMAL, a mountainous district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Salem, between 12° and 14° N lat., lying to the SE of the Western Ghauts, and separated from Coimbatur by the Caveri river. The pop. are chiefly Hindus. Its staple products are wheat, maize, tobacco, and sugar.
BARRAN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Gers, cant. and 8 m. WSW of D'Auch. Pop. 1,820.
BARRAN. See BARREN.
BARRANCA, a town of Peru, on the sea-coast, NW of Lima.—Also a town of New Granada, on the l. bank of the Magdalena, 42 m. ESE of Carthagena.
BARRASFORD, a township in the p. of Chollerton, Northumberland, 6 m. N of Hexham. Pop. 209.

BARRAUX, a commune and fort of France, in the dep. of Isere, cant. of Touvet, near the W bank of the Isere, and at the entrance of the valley of Gresivaudan, I m. from the frontier of Savoy. Pop. 1,452. The fort, situated on the road from Grenoble to Chamberry, 22 m. NW of the former, occupies a commanding position, and is of considerable strength.
BARRAX, or BARAX, a town of Spain, in New Castile, and prov. of La Mancha, 26 m. NE of Alcaraz, and 80 m. ENE of Ciudad Real. Pop. 1,500.
BARRAY. See BARRA.

BARRE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Lozere, arrond. of Florac. The cant. comprises 8 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 5,996. The town is 7 m. SSE of Florac. There is a consistorial Calvinistic church here. Pop. 1,034. -Also a township of Vermont, U. S., in Washington co., 48 m. NW of Windsor. The Onion river, by which it is watered, affords good water-power. In the immediate vicinity of the town are extensive quarries of granite. Pop. in 1840, 2,126.—Also a township of Pennsylvania, U. S., in Huntingdon co. Pop. in 1840, 2,225.—Also a township in Massachusetts, U.S., in Worcester co., 65 m. W of Boston. Its soil is generally fertile, and its pasturage luxuriant. The Ware river, by which and its tributaries it is watered, affords good water-power. Pop. in 1840. 2,751.—Also a township of New York, U. S., in Orleans co., 252 m. W of Albany. It presents a hilly surface, with a generally fertile loamy soil, and is intersected by the Erie canal. Pop. 5,539. The principal village is Albion or Barre Centre.
BARRE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Haute Vienne, and com. of Veyrac. Pop. 328 .- Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, and cant. of Beaumesnil. Pop. 1,062.

BARRE-DE-MONT (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Vendee, and com. of Notre-Dame-de- Mont, near the entrance of the Perrier canal into the bay of Bourgneuf. Pop. 400. It possesses a small port, the principal exports of which are grain and salt. The shipping in 1837 amounted to 3,877 tons, and the exports, chiefly in grain, meal, and pulse, in 1839, to 7,222 quintals.
BARREA, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Ultra 2da, in the district and 20 m. S of Sulmona, on the r. bank of the Sangro, in a mountainous district. It has an hospital. Pop. 1,500.

BARREAH, a town of Hindostan, the cap. of a small principality of the same name, in the prov. of Gujerat, 90 m. ENE of Cambay, in N lat. 22° 44′, E long. 74°. It is situated in a valley, in a narrow space stretching along the northern bank of the Paunaum, and consists of neatly-built houses-in a large proportion of brick. The territory is, in almost its entire extent, an uncultivated jungly waste, and the pop. scanty, composed of nomadic tribes, subsisting on rapine and pillage. The revenue of the rajah, which in 1819 amounted to 57,000 rupees, is chiefly derived from the chouts or duties on trade levied on the adjacent districts.
BARREDO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Alava, 20 m. WSW of Vittoria, near the l. bank of the Ebro.
BARREE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, and pergunnah of Buggelwarrah, on a tributary of the Nerbudda, 47 m. ESE of Bhopal.
BARREGES. See BAREGES.
